questions.stem: The graphical solution method for a linear program is applicable when the model has:
questions.explanation: The graphical method works only for LP problems with two decision variables, because the feasible region can then be drawn on a plane. Problems with more variables require algebraic methods such as simplex.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: The feasible region of a two-variable LP is found by:
options: Plotting all constraints and shading the area satisfying them simultaneously
questions.explanation: Each constraint is plotted as a line and the half-plane it allows is identified; the feasible region is the common shaded area satisfying every constraint and the non-negativity conditions at once. The objective is drawn only afterward to locate the optimum.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: According to the fundamental theorem used in the graphical method, the optimal solution of an LP (when it exists and is finite) occurs at:
options: The centre of the feasible region
A point outside the feasible region
An extreme (corner) point of the feasible region
questions.explanation: The collection of feasible solutions forms a convex set whose extreme points are the basic feasible solutions, and at least one extreme point yields an optimum. Hence the corner-point method evaluates the objective only at the vertices.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: In the extreme-point method one must evaluate the objective function at:
options: Every corner point of the feasible region
questions.explanation: The extreme-point method computes the objective value at each corner of the feasible region and compares them; the vertex giving the best value is optimal. Interior points and edge midpoints are never optimal corners.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: For Maximize $Z = 15x_1 + 10x_2$ over a feasible region with corners $O(0,0), A(60,0), B(60,20), C(30,40), D(0,40)$, the objective value at $B$ is:
questions.explanation: At $B(60,20)$, $Z = 15(60) + 10(20) = 900 + 200 = 1100$. Evaluating every corner gives $O = 0$, $A = 900$, $B = 1100$, $C = 850$, $D = 400$, so $B$ attains the largest value.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: For the same corners with Maximize $Z = 15x_1 + 10x_2$, which corner is optimal?
questions.explanation: Comparing the corner values $0, 900, 1100, 850, 400$, the maximum is $1100$ at $B(60,20)$, so $B$ is the optimal extreme point.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: At the corner $C(30,40)$, the value of $Z = 15x_1 + 10x_2$ is:
questions.explanation: Substituting gives $Z = 15(30) + 10(40) = 450 + 400 = 850$. This is less than the value $1100$ at corner $B$, so $C$ is not optimal.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: For Minimize $Z = -x_1 + 2x_2$ with feasible corners $O(0,0), A(2,0), B(4,2), C(2,4), D(0,10/3)$, the minimum value is:
questions.explanation: The corner values are $O = 0$, $A = -1(2) + 2(0) = -2$, $B = 0$, $C = 6$, and $D = 20/3$. The smallest is $-2$ at $A(2,0)$, so that corner gives the optimal (minimum) solution.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: An iso-profit (or iso-cost) line is best described as:
options: A line along which the objective function has a constant value
A constraint boundary of the feasible region
The line joining the two most distant corners
questions.explanation: An iso-profit line is a set of points all giving the same objective value; by assigning different values to $Z$ one gets a family of parallel lines. The optimum is found by sliding this line as far as the objective improves while still touching the feasible region.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: In the iso-profit line method, to maximize the objective you move the iso-profit line:
options: Toward the origin until it leaves the region
Parallel to itself in the direction of increasing objective value until it last touches the feasible region
Until it passes through the origin
questions.explanation: The iso-profit line is shifted parallel to itself in the direction that increases $Z$; the last corner (or edge) it touches before leaving the feasible region is the optimal point. For minimization it is moved in the decreasing direction.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: To decide which side of a plotted constraint is feasible, a convenient test is to check whether:
questions.explanation: Substitute the origin $(0,0)$ into the constraint: if it is satisfied, the feasible side is the one containing the origin; if not, the feasible side is away from the origin. This origin test quickly orients each half-plane.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: The set of all feasible solutions of an LP forms a convex set. 'Convex' means that:
options: The region has at least one hole
The straight segment joining any two points of the region lies entirely within it
questions.explanation: A set is convex when, for any two of its points, the entire line segment between them stays inside the set. The feasible region of an LP, being an intersection of half-planes, is such a convex polygon (polyhedron).
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: Each corner (extreme) point of the feasible region of a two-variable LP occurs at:
options: The intersection of two constraint boundary lines
A point where the objective is zero
questions.explanation: A vertex of the feasible region is formed where two constraint equalities (boundary lines) intersect. These intersection points are exactly the basic feasible solutions evaluated in the corner-point method.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: If, when sliding the iso-profit line, it can move away from the constraints without limit, the LP has:
questions.explanation: When the iso-profit line keeps improving the objective without ever leaving the feasible region, the objective is unbounded. The slides note this usually signals an error in formulating the model.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: If the iso-profit line at its optimum coincides with one entire edge (boundary line) of the feasible region, then:
options: There are multiple (alternative) optimal solutions along that edge
questions.explanation: When the highest iso-profit line lies along a boundary edge, every point on that edge gives the same optimal objective value, so the LP has alternative optimal solutions, found at the two adjoining corners and the points between them.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: At the corner $A(60,0)$ for Maximize $Z = 15x_1 + 10x_2$, the objective value is:
questions.explanation: Substituting gives $Z = 15(60) + 10(0) = 900$. This is below the optimum $1100$ at $B$, so corner $A$ is feasible but not optimal.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: Which statement about a two-variable LP feasible region is correct?
options: It has a finite number of basic feasible (corner) solutions
It can have infinitely many corner points
questions.explanation: There are only finitely many extreme points (basic feasible solutions) within the feasible region. This is why evaluating the objective at each corner is a finite, workable procedure.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: A good arbitrary value of $Z$ at which to first draw an iso-profit line is one that:
options: Makes the line pass through the optimal corner immediately
Gives convenient integer intercepts on both axes
Equals zero so the line is the origin
questions.explanation: It is practical to pick a $Z$ value that yields whole-number intercepts when each variable is set to zero in turn, often a number divisible by both objective coefficients, making the initial iso-profit line easy to plot.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: At the corner $D(0,40)$ for Maximize $Z = 15x_1 + 10x_2$, the objective value is:
questions.explanation: Substituting gives $Z = 15(0) + 10(40) = 400$. This is the smallest of the maximization corner values, so $D$ is far from optimal.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: In the corner-point method, a feasible solution that lies strictly inside the feasible region (not on a vertex) is:
options: Never the optimal solution for a linear objective with a finite optimum
questions.explanation: For a linear objective with a finite optimum, the best value is always attained at an extreme point, so an interior point can be improved by moving toward a vertex and is never uniquely optimal. This justifies checking only the corners.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: If a corner point of a two-variable LP is found by intersecting $3x_1 = 180$ and $4x_1 + 6x_2 = 360$, the coordinates are:
questions.explanation: From $3x_1 = 180$ we get $x_1 = 60$; substituting into $4(60) + 6x_2 = 360$ gives $240 + 6x_2 = 360$, so $6x_2 = 120$ and $x_2 = 20$. The intersection corner is $(60, 20)$.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: The two graphical solution approaches discussed in the slides are the extreme-point method and the:
questions.explanation: The slides present two graphical approaches: the extreme-point (corner) method and the iso-profit or iso-cost line method. The Big-M, stepping-stone, and Hungarian methods belong to simplex, transportation, and assignment problems respectively.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: Maximize $Z = 15x_1 + 10x_2$ over the feasible region with corners $O(0,0), A(60,0), B(60,20), C(30,40), D(0,40)$.
questions.solution: Evaluating the objective at $A(60,0)$ gives $Z =$ {{1}}, and at $B(60,20)$ gives $Z =$ {{2}}. Comparing all five corners, the maximum value is {{3}}, attained at corner $B$.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.solution: At corner $A(2,0)$ the objective equals {{1}}. Since this is the smallest corner value, the optimal solution occurs at $A$, and the problem is being {{2}}.
questions.explanation: At $A(2,0)$: $Z = -1(2) + 2(0) = -2$, the smallest among $0, -2, 0, 6, 20/3$, so $A$ is the minimizing corner.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: The graphical method relies on properties of the feasible region.
questions.solution: The feasible region of a linear program is a {{1}} set, meaning the segment joining any two of its points stays inside it. The optimal value of a linear objective is attained at an {{2}} point of this region, where two constraint boundary lines intersect.
questions.explanation: An LP feasible region is convex, and an optimal solution lies at an extreme (corner) point formed by intersecting constraint lines.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: To plot a constraint and orient its feasible side, the origin test is used.
questions.solution: Substitute the point $(0,$ {{1}} $)$ into the constraint. If the origin satisfies the inequality, the feasible region lies on the side {{2}} the origin; otherwise it lies away from the origin.
questions.explanation: Test the origin $(0,0)$: if it satisfies the constraint the feasible half-plane contains the origin, otherwise it is on the far side.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: Find the corner where $3x_1 = 180$ meets $4x_1 + 6x_2 = 360$.
questions.solution: From the first equation $x_1 =$ {{1}}. Substituting into the second gives $240 + 6x_2 = 360$, so $x_2 =$ {{2}}. The corner point is therefore $(60, 20)$.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research

questions.stem: The iso-profit line method slides a line of constant objective value across the feasible region.
questions.solution: For a maximization problem the iso-profit line is moved parallel to itself in the direction of {{1}} objective value until it last touches the region. If at the optimum the line coincides with an entire edge, the problem has {{2}} optimal solutions.
questions.explanation: Maximize by sliding the iso-profit line in the increasing direction to the last touching corner; if it lies along a whole edge there are multiple (alternative) optima.
questions.source: in the style of Sharma, Operations Research
